key: sperber|1981|irony and the use mention dist
bare: - Sperber, D. & Wilson, D. (1981). "Irony and the Use-Mention Distinction." In P. Cole (ed.), *Radical Pragmatics*. Academic Press. [PDF](https://www.dan.sperber.fr/wp-content/uploads/1984_verbal-irony-pretense-or-echoic-mention.pdf)

key: sperber|1981|used the term echoic mention b
bare: Sperber and Wilson initially (1981) used the term "echoic mention" but later (1986, 2012) refined this to "echoic use," broadening the notion. They emphasized that the echo need not be of a specific prior utterance -- it can echo an expectation, a hope, a social norm, or any thought attributable to someone. This made the theory more flexible and able to handle cases where there is no obvious prior statement being echoed.

1. Sperber and Wilson's (1986) Relevance Theory makes the role of **intention recognition** explicit in communication. Understanding what someone means requires recognizing what they *intended* to communicate -- and this requires the ability to attribute mental states.
- Sperber, D. (1994). "Understanding verbal understanding." In J. Khalfa (Ed.), *What is Intelligence?*. Cambridge University Press, 179-198. [Sperber website](https://www.dan.sperber.fr/?p=19)
- [Sperber & Wilson (2002). "Relevance Theory Revisited"](https://people.bu.edu/bfraser/Relevance%20Theory%20Oriented/Sperber%20&%20Wilson%20-%20RT%20Revisited.pdf)
- Sperber, D., et al. (2010). "Epistemic Vigilance." *Mind & Language*, 25(4), 359-393. [UCL](https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/1331363/1/Wilson_Epistemic%20Vigilance%20revised%20final.pdf)

- Trust and Deception in Children with Autism Spectrum Disorders (2016): limited social engagement may result in failure to learn social cues indicating deceit.

Uisma, Virkki & Ylilahti (2026) empirically document how "often-intertwined forms of epistemic injustices are related to a lack of representativeness in how autism is understood as well as to how the exclusion of certain kinds of knowledge and knowers, along with the devaluations of expressive styles, impact autistic people's epistemic agency, identities and access to care."

- Uisma, A.-M., Virkki, T. & Ylilahti, M. (2026). Misunderstood, minimised, misrepresented: Autistic young adults' experiences of epistemic injustices in healthcare interactions around autism. *Sociology of Health & Illness*.

- Ullman, S. E. (2010). *Talking About Sexual Assault: Society's Response to Survivors.* American Psychological Association. — [Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Talking-About-Sexual-Assault-Psychology/dp/1433808072)
- Ullman, S. E., & Peter-Hagene, L. (2014). "Social reactions to sexual assault disclosure, coping, perceived control, and PTSD symptoms in sexual assault victims." *Journal of Community Psychology.*
